claim: The recipes section (25 folios, 11,611 words, entropy 3.8586 bits â€” the largest section by word count) encodes a natural-language text with less cipher transformation than the herbal section, as evidenced by its higher entropy and larger word count approximating the statistical footprint of an unenciphered medieval recipe corpus.
evidence_for: Recipes section has second-highest entropy (3.8586 bits), just below text-only (3.9016), suggesting less entropy-reducing cipher transformation
At 11,611 words it is the largest section, providing the most statistical power for language identification
High belief (0.88) that recipes section is closest to natural language among illustrated sections
evidence_against: Entropy of 3.8586 is still below the text-only section, so some cipher transformation may remain
Recipes sections in medieval manuscripts use highly formulaic, repetitive language which independently lowers entropy â€” this could confound the comparison
Without a decoded passage, language identification from entropy alone is ambiguous between Latin, Italian, and other Romance languages
test: Compare the bigram and trigram glyph entropy of the recipes section against (a) text-only section, (b) herbal section, and (c) a synthetic corpus of medieval Latin recipes encoded under simple substitution; compute mutual information between adjacent word pairs in the recipes section and compare to herbal.
test_metric: Bigram conditional entropy H(g2|g1) and trigram conditional entropy H(g3|g2,g1) for recipes vs. other sections; inter-word mutual information
test_threshold: Recipes bigram entropy within 0.05 bits of text-only section AND significantly above herbal would confirm recipes as least-enciphered section; inter-word MI > herbal MI would indicate more grammatical structure.
